lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: You just proved $A$ from the assumption $A$. A trifle, perhaps — but look closer.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: What is there to see? We borrowed what we needed and gave it back.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Precisely. But now imagine: you assume something — call it $P$ — and, reasoning from that assumption, you reach a conclusion $Q$.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: I have $P$ in the context, and I derive $Q$. So what?

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: So you have shown something deeper: **if** $P$ **then** $Q$. The assumption was only borrowed. It does not need to be true in the world — only in the argument.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: So the proof itself becomes the implication. I discharge the assumption $P$ and package the whole derivation into $P \to Q$.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: That is `(->-intro F N)`: from step $N$ — which proved the consequent under the assumption $F$ — form $F \to$ (the consequent). The assumption vanishes from the context.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: And the reverse? If I have $P \to Q$ and also $P$?

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Then `(->-elim N M)` — [modus-ponens|modus ponens] — lets you detach $Q$. Step $N$ gives the arrow, step $M$ gives the antecedent. Out comes the consequent.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: Introduction builds the arrow. Elimination uses it. Two sides of the same coin.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Now prove it. Start with the simplest implication of all: if you are yourself, then you are yourself.
